event: not completely dependent on the ventilator
never left the bed or nursing chair
stenosis of the internal axis carotis on both sides
esophageal stenosis was dilated in a secondary hospital
referred to the University of Erlangen
fistula between the esophagus and tracheal membrane had occurred in the upper third of the trachea
examined by Ear, Nose and Throat, Thoracic Surgery, Pulmonology and Medical intensive care unit
minimal invasive orthograde approach would be impossible
admitted to the surgical intensive care unit
pneumonia by 4-multiresistente gramnegative Pseudomonas aeruginosa in the right lung
ventilated through a tracheostoma with low ventilation forces
could not open her mouth over a maximum of 20 degrees
thoracic computed tomography confirmed a big fistula of the tracheal membrane of at least 3.5 cm length
tracheal cannula ended shortly beneath the lower limit of the mediastinal fistula
plan to close the fistula with a pedicled omentum majus replacement
written informed consent for publication provided
vv-ECMO began to be partly ineffective due to rising septical issues
high volume input of physiological saline was needed
endobronchial stenting performed retrograde via the percutaneous tracheostoma
tried different Dumon and one-hybrid self expandable metallic y-stent
mandatory additional ventilation through a nasal jet catheter
double-lumen endotracheal tube exchange catheter (DLET) used
new stoma created in the FS and fixed subcutaneously
Freitag stent 11 cm length and inner diameter 13 mm placed successfully retrograde bridging the fistula
regular tracheal cannula introduced for ventilation
could communicate with her family by writing and her eyes
infections continued to be very severe
reduction of intravenous saline injection limited due to the mandatory but reduced vv-ECMO support
spontaneous work of breathing never exceeded a tidal ventilation of 170 mL per breath
decided actively to reduce the vv-ECMO support
